God showed his presence among the Israelites in a tabernacle or tent, because of their situation in the wilderness. God adjusts the signs of his favor and the gifts of his grace to match his people's state and needs. The curtains of the tabernacle were to be very rich. They were to be embroidered with cherubim, signifying that the angels of God pitch their tents around the church.

The curtains of lesser materials, being made both longer and broader, covered the others, and were protected by coverings of skins. The whole represents the person and doctrine of Christ, and the church of true Christians, and all heavenly things, which outwardly are plain, but inwardly, and in God's sight, are glorious and precious.

The sockets of silver each weighed about 115 pounds; they were placed in rows on the ground. In every pair of these sockets, a strong board of shittim-wood, covered with plates of gold, was fitted by mortises and tenons. Thus walls were formed for the two sides, and for the west end. The wall was further held together by bars, which passed through rings of gold. Over this the curtains were spread. Though movable, it was strong and firm. The materials were very costly. In all this it was a type of the church of God, built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief Cornerstone.

A vail, or curtain, separated the holy place from the most holy place. It was hung upon pillars. This vail was for a partition between the holy place and the most holy; which forbade anyone to look into the holiest of all. The apostle explains what this vail meant. That the ceremonial law could not make those who came thereunto perfect, nor would the observance of it bring people to heaven; the way into the holiest of all was not made clear while the first tabernacle was standing. Life and immortality lay hidden till they were brought to light by the gospel; which was signified by the rending of this vail at the death of Christ. We now have boldness to enter into the holiest, in all acts of worship, by the blood of Jesus; yet it obliges us to holy reverence. Another vail was for the outer door of the tabernacle. This vail was all the defense the tabernacle had. God takes care of his church on earth. A curtain will be, if God pleases to make it so, as strong a defense to his house, as gates of brass and bars of iron. With this typical description of Christ and his church before us, what is our judgment of these matters? Do we see any glory in the person of Christ? any excellence in his character? any thing precious in his salvation? or any wisdom in the doctrine of the cross? Will our religion bear examination? and are we more careful to approve our hearts to God than our characters toward people?
